coredata.dc:title: Micelle-induced assembly of graphene quantum dots into conductive porous carbon for high rate supercapacitor electrodes at high mass loadings
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Wenhui
Zhu, Jiayao
Dong, Yue
Zhao, Jing
Li, Jing
Guo, Nannan
Lin, He
Zhang, Su
Jia, Dianzeng
coredata.dc:description: Achieving high rate capability of porous carbon at high mass loading is important for developing advanced supercapacitors. But it still remains a big challenge because thick electrode causes severely reduced electric conductivity and blocked ion migration channels. Herein, we develop a micelle-induced assembly method to prepare conductive porous carbon through puzzling flexible graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The unique sp2 hybridized GQDs ensure the product with a two times higher electric conductivity than the commercial activated carbon. The interconnected mesoporous structure promotes robust ion transport kinetics especially at high mass loadings. As supercapacitor electrode, it shows high capacitances of 315 and 170 F g−1 at 1 and 100 A g−1, respectively. Importantly, at a very high mass loading of 20 mg cm−2, it shows a remarkably high areal capacitances of 2.8 F cm−2 at 10 A g−1, which is much better than other reported carbon materials. The symmetric supercapacitors show maximum energy densities of 9.21 and 6.45 Wh kg−1 at the mass loadings of 2 mg cm−2 and 20 mg cm−2, respectively, revealing the structural advantage of GQD-puzzled porous carbon for practical applications.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Education, Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a1) CPC-0, (b1) CPC-0.3, and (c1) CPC-0.5. HRTEM images of (a2 and a3) CPC-0, (b2 and b3) CPC-0.3, and (c2 and c3) CPC-0.5. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, (b) specific surface areas and porous structure evolution, (c) XRD patterns, (d) Raman spectra, (e) XPS survey spectra, and (f) C1s spectra of the CPC-0, CPC-0.3, CPC-0.5, and CPC-0.8. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical performance in three-electrode system: (a) conductivity of CPC-0, CPC-0.3, CPC-0.5, CPC-0.8, commercial activated carbon, and conductive carbon black. (b) CV curves at 10 mV s−1, (c) galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles at 1 A g−1, and (d) the rate performance of CPC-0, CPC-0.3, CPC-0.5, CPC-0.8, and commercial activated carbon. (e) Specific areal capacitance of CPC-0.3 versus the reported carbon materials. (f) Nyquist plots of CPC-0, CPC-0.3, CPC-0.5, and CPC-0.8. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical performance of CPC-0.3 at different mass loadings: (a) CV curves at 10 mV s−1, (b) galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles at 1 A g−1, and (c) specific capacitances at 1 and 10 A g−1 in three-electrode system. (d) Comparison of electrode areal capacitance of CPC-0.3 with other materials at 10 A g−1. (e) Nyquist plots and (f) Bode phase plots of CPC-0.3 at different mass loadings. (g) Ragone plots of the CPC-0.3//CPC-0.3 symmetric supercapacitors at different mass loadings comparison with other carbon materials. (h) Cycle performance at 10 A g−1 of the CPC-0.3//CPC-0.3 symmetric device at 10 mg cm−2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The illustration of preparation route and structure of the CPCs.

head.ce:title: Micelle-induced assembly of graphene quantum dots into conductive porous carbon for high rate supercapacitor electrodes at high mass loadings

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830046, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Energy Materials Chemistry, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830046, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering
Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Process and Technology for Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Achieving high rate capability of porous carbon at high mass loading is important for developing advanced supercapacitors. But it still remains a big challenge because thick electrode causes severely reduced electric conductivity and blocked ion migration channels. Herein, we develop a micelle-induced assembly method to prepare conductive porous carbon through puzzling flexible graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The unique sp2 hybridized GQDs ensure the product with a two times higher electric conductivity than the commercial activated carbon. The interconnected mesoporous structure promotes robust ion transport kinetics especially at high mass loadings. As supercapacitor electrode, it shows high capacitances of 315 and 170 F g−1 at 1 and 100 A g−1, respectively. Importantly, at a very high mass loading of 20 mg cm−2, it shows a remarkably high areal capacitances of 2.8 F cm−2 at 10 A g−1, which is much better than other reported carbon materials. The symmetric supercapacitors show maximum energy densities of 9.21 and 6.45 Wh kg−1 at the mass loadings of 2 mg cm−2 and 20 mg cm−2, respectively, revealing the structural advantage of GQD-puzzled porous carbon for practical applications.

Conductive porous carbon prepared by micelle-induced assembly of graphene quantum dots shows high capacitive and rate performance at high mass loadings due to sufficient ion and electron transport paths.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have been widely used as high-power devices in portable electronics, power backups, and electric vehicles owing to their fast charge-discharge ability and long lifespan [1–4]. In most of the practical cases, supercapacitors should be installed in a limited space. Thus device miniaturization and lightweight to satisfy efficient space utilization is very important for future development. To meet this requirement, the active mass loading on electrodes should be increased as high as possible to improve the total energy density of the devices [5,6]. Although high specific capacitance is obtained for most pseudocapacitive materials such as metal oxides and conductive polymers, it is hardly realized especially at high mass loadings because of the poor intrinsic conductivity and sluggish electrochemical kinetics [4]. By contrast, porous carbon materials with a robust electrical double-layer behavior show great potential in this field. For instance, several advanced carbon materials such as hierarchically porous carbons [7–9], carbon fiber fabrics [10], and graphene films [11–14] have been developed for delivering high areal capacitance at high mass loadings. Unfortunately, most of the carbon materials still confront with the rapidly reduced rate performance due to the decreased conductivity and blocked ion migration channels with the rising of mass loadings, which severely hinders their practical applications.

To solve this problem, the desired porous carbon should simultaneously possess good electric conductivity and robust ion migration kinetics. However, there is a trade-off between the electron and ion transport paths for carbon materials. From the structural aspect, meso- and macro-porous carbons with sufficient ion migration channels can be easily constructed through template-assisted methods [15–19]. However, well-developed pores will break the large-area conjugated networks, resulting in the products with low electric conductivity. For thick electrodes, the conductivity issue becomes serious to restrict effective utilization of active materials as well as good rate performance [20]. As a result, it is still challenging to prepare conductive porous carbon for high power electrode at high mass loadings. In recent works, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were found to show great potential for preparing conductive porous carbon because of their unique sp2 hybridized structure with excellent dispersibility and flexibility [21–23]. The GQD-derived carbon materials such as porous graphene [24], ultramicroporous carbons [25], activated carbon spheres [26], and carbon nanosheets [27] exhibit outstanding capacitive and rate performance.

Inspired by these works, herein, a highly conductive porous carbon (CPC) was prepared through a simple micelle-induced assembly method using crystallized GQDs as precursors and block copolymer as soft template. The CPC shows an interconnected mesoporous structure with both high specific surface area of 1323 m−2 g−1 and electric conductivity of 73 S m−1, enabling fast electron and ion transport especially at high mass loadings. When tested by three-electrode system, it shows a high capacitance of 315 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and good rate capability of 54% capacitance retention at 100 A g−1. Importantly, at a very high mass loading of 20 mg cm−2, it shows a remarkably high areal capacitances of 2.8 F cm−2 at 10 A g−1, which is much better than other reported carbon materials. Moreover, the symmetric supercapacitors show maximum energy densities of 9.21 and 6.45 Wh kg−1 at mass loadings of 2 mg cm−2 and 20 mg cm−2, respectively, revealing the structural advantage of GQD-puzzled porous carbon for practical applications.
GQDs were prepared in large-scale through a chemical oxidation method using bituminous coal as the precursor (Heishan, Xinjiang, China). The details are seen in our previous works [25–28].

The preparation procedure of the CPCs is shown in Scheme 1 . In a typical run, 0.5 g GQDs and 0.5 g KOH were completely dispersed in 20 mL ethanol to prepare solution A. Meanwhile, 0.3 g Pluronic triblock copolymer (EO106PO70EO106, F127, Mw = 12600) was dissolved in 40 mL ethanol to prepare solution B. The above two solutions were mixed by vigorous stirring for 6 h. After being dried at 80 °C and milled to fine powder, the mixture was carbonized at 800 °C for 1 h in N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1. The product was washed using diluted HCl (1 mol L−1) to remove the inorganic salt, and then washed to neutral using deionized water. The samples prepared with different F127 dosage were named as CPC-X, where “X” stands for the mass of F127.

Morphology of the samples was measured by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2100F). The samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance), Raman spectroscopy (Bruker SENTERRA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250 Xi), and N2 adsorption-desorption isothermal method (ASAP 2020, Micromeritics). The electric conductivity of the samples was obtained from the I–V curves. The details are given in our previous work [26].

The working electrode was prepared as follows: 85 wt% of the sample, 10 wt% of conductive carbon black, and 5 wt% of polytetrafluoroethylene were milled to slurry using ethanol as the solvent, then coated onto the nickel foam (1 × 1 cm2) as current collector with the mass loadings of 2, 5, 10, 15, and 20 mg cm−2. Aqueous KOH electrolyte (6 mol L−1) was used in both three- and two-electrode systems according to our previous works [25,26]. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge experiments were performed on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China). The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were recorded at the open-circuit potential in the frequency range of 0.01–100,000 Hz with a modulating amplitude of 5 mV. The gravimetric specific capacitance (C, F g−1) was calculated from the charge-discharge curves using the following equation [7]:
(1)
C =
I Δ t
m Δ V
where I (A) is the discharge current, Δt (s) refers to the discharge time, and ΔV (V) corresponds to the potential/voltage window. m (g) is the mass of active material loaded on a single working electrode.

The specific areal capacitance (C
BET , F m−2) was calculated by the following equation:
(2)
C
B E T
=
C
S
B E T
where S
BET (m2 g−1) is the specific surface area.

The electrode areal capacitance (C
e , F cm−2) was calculated by the following equation:
(3)
C
e
=
C M
1000
where M (mg cm−2) is the mass of active material loaded on per square centimeter on a single working electrode.

The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) based on the electrode were calculated based on the following equations [3,7,29]:
(4)
E =
C Δ
V
2
8
× 3.6
(5)
P =
3600 E
Δ t
where ΔV (V) represents the voltage range of the device excluding the IR drop.

F127 is widely used as soft template for preparing mesoporous carbon materials due to the easy self-assembly to micelle in solution [30–34]. GQDs used in this work show a highly crystallized structure with abundant oxygen-bearing functional groups [25–28], ensuring good compatibility with the surfactant F127. CPC-0 without using F127 shows a block appearance with some macropores in between them (Fig. 1a1 ). The HRTEM images (Fig. 1a2 and 1a3) clearly indicate the compact inner structure of CPC-0 puzzled by densely packed GQDs. As seen in Fig. 1b1, CPC-0.3 prepared by micelle-induced assembly of GQDs exhibits an ant nest structure with abundant interconnected meso- and macro-pores. The thin-walled bubble-like mesopores have a size distribution ranging from 30 to 50 nm (Fig. 1b2), facilitating electrolyte infiltration and fast ion transport. Importantly, from the zoomed HRTEM image given in Fig. 1b3, many small crystal domains (marked by the arrows) are observed in the CPC-0.3, revealing its high electric conductivity. By increasing the F127 dosage, the size of the assembled micelles is enlarged, leading to the CPC-0.5 with abundant macropores and many broken pieces inside the pores (Fig. 1c1 and 1c2). The mesoporous ant nest structure of CPC-0.3 is collapsed. Interestingly, no obvious crystalline domains are observed in Fig. 1c3. The reason is that the surfactant F127 also produces some amorphous carbon during carbonization. Excessive amorphous carbon and over-developed meso- and macro-porous structure result in discontinuous sp2 conjugated structure and decreased conductivity.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were further used to characterize the porous structure of the samples (Fig. 2 a). The isotherms can be divided into three major processes occurring in the low (P/P0 < 0.1), middle (0.1 < P/P0 < 0.9), and high (P/P0 > 0.9) relative pressure regions, corresponding to the presence of micro-, meso-, and macropores, respectively. The CPCs exhibit a type I/IV isotherm with a rapid adsorption in the low pressure region, meaning the formation of many micropores due to the in-situ activation process. The hysteresis loops in the middle pressure region are clearly observed, demonstrating the existence of large numbers of mesopores [31]. The mesopores volume first increases from 0.096 cm3 g−1 of the CPC-0 to 0.370 cm3 g−1 of the CPC-0.5 and then decreases to 0.019 cm3 g−1 of the CPC-0.8 (Fig. 2b, Fig. S1, and Table S1), indicating that the mesopore volume is well controlled by F127 dosage from CPC-0 to CPC-0.5. Excessive F127 results in the collapse of mesoporous structure and the formation of abundant macropores in CPCs during micelle-induced assembly, as observed by the SEM and HRTEM images of CPC-0.5 (Fig. 1c1-1c3) and CPC-0.8 (Fig. S1). As the mesoporous ant nest structure is collapsed in CPC-0.5, the high mesopore volume of CPC-0.5 is originated from the residual mesopores in the particles and the stacking mesopores between the particles (Fig. 1c1). What’s more, the specific surface area and micropore volume of the CPCs gradually decrease from 1902 m2 g−1 and 0.667 cm3 g−1 of CPC-0 to 1218 m2 g−1 and 0.019 cm3 g−1 of CPC-0.8 with the increasing of F127 dosage. The linear F127 molecules have higher chemical reactivity than the sp2 hybridized GQDs. They will consume KOH activator during carbonization, leading to the decreased micropore volume. It should be mentioned that although CPC-0.3 has lower mesopores volume than CPC-0.5, the average mesopores size of CPC-0.3 is larger than that of the CPC-0.5 (Fig. S2) due to the complete ant nest structure without collapse, which is favourable for the fast ion and electron transport.

The XRD patterns of the samples are shown in Fig. 2c. The samples exhibit a dispersive diffraction peaks at approximately 44° (100), indicating the random packing of GQDs in the samples. Compared with CPC-0, an obvious rising in the low-angle scattering region of CPC-0.3, CPC-0.5, and CPC-0.8 further proves the presence of mesopores [31,35–37], which is in agreement with the SEM and HRTEM images. Another interesting thing is that although the CPC-0 shows the highest specific surface area and micropore volume, its ID/IG value (0.84) in Raman spectrum (Fig. 2d) is lower than CPC-0.3 (0.93). This reveals that the high defect density [38,39] of CPC-0.3 is mainly coming from the interconnected mesoporous structure with enriched edge sites. In addition, the ID/IG values of CPC-0.5 and CPC-0.8 are lower than CPC-0.3 due to the collapsed structure by excessive F127. The XPS measurements were carried out to disclose the elemental composition and chemical state of the samples. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 2e) show the intense C1s peak at 284.8 eV and the O1s peak at 531.9 eV. The C1s spectra (Fig. 2f) can be divided into three peaks located at around 284.5, 285.8, and 287.9 eV, corresponding to the CC, C–O, and CO components, respectively [25–27]. The O/C ratio and C–O content (Table S2) of the CPC-0.3 are slightly higher than those of the other samples also due to the appropriate mesoporous structure with abundant edge sites.

Conductivity of carbon materials is crucial for supercapacitor electrodes especially at high mass loadings. In general, large surface area and well-developed ion migration channels are contradicted with high conductivity of carbon electrode materials because the pores can break the large area conjugated structure. In this work, the CPC-0 exhibits a conductivity of 85 S m−1, which is close to the conductive carbon black of 105 S m−1 and two times higher than the amorphous commercial activated carbon of 39 S m−1 (Fig. 3 a, Fig. S3). By introducing meso- and macro-pores using F127 surfactant, the conductivity gradually decreases to 73 S m−1 for CPC-0.3, 71 S m−1 for CPC-0.5, and 54 S m−1 for CPC-0.8, but still much higher than that of the commercial activated carbon. The result demonstrates the advantage of sp2 hybridized GQDs for constructing highly conductive porous carbon materials. The CPC-0 (without F127) is totally built up by crystallized GQDs, ensuring abundant electron transport paths. The gradually decreased conductivity along with the increase of F127 dosage is because (1) the over-developed meso- and macro-pores break the continuous conductive network (Fig. 1c1 and Figure S1a1), and (2) pyrolysis of large amount of F127 molecules results in the formation of amorphous carbon in between the crystallized GQDs (Fig. 1c3 and Figure S1c3).

The CV curves at 10 mV s−1 (Fig. 3b) and galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles at 1 A g−1 (Fig. 3c) of the samples exhibit approximate rectangular and isosceles triangular shapes, respectively, indicating the electrical double-layer behavior. The CPC-0.3 exhibits the largest CV integral area and the longest discharge time, demonstrating the highest capacitance among all the samples due to the complete ant nest like structure, which allows the full utilization of the surface area and active sites. By contrast, the complete structure is collapsed due to the formation of abundant macropores by excessive F127. The particles are loosely stacked together in CPC-0.5 (Fig. 1c1) and CPC-0.8 (Fig. S1), and some blocks are even separated from each other, resulting in the blocked electron transport and incomplete utilization of active components. Therefore, the CPC-0.5 with higher mesopore volume and comparable conductivity to CPC-0.3 shows slight lower specific capacitances. The small IR drops of CPC-0.3 even at high current densities reveal its high power ability (Fig. S4). The CPC-0.3 shows the highest capacitances of 315, 212, and 170 F g−1 at 1, 50, and 100 A g−1 among the samples (Fig. 3d). The capacitance retention remains 54% at 100 A g−1, indicating its good rate performance (Table S3). What’s more, the specific areal capacitance (capacitance per BET surface area) of the CPC-0.3 is 0.24 F m−2 (Fig. 3e), which is higher than most of the reported carbon materials [7,40–48] such as large-surface-area activated carbons, porous carbons, and graphene derivations. The result indicates that the CPC-0.3 has abundant electron and ion transport paths, promoting the effective utilization of the surface area.

The Nyquist plots of the samples further confirm the robust electrochemical kinetics of CPC-0.3 for supercapacitors. As seen in Fig. 3f, all of the curves consist of a semicircle in the high frequency region and a line in the low frequency region. The plots of CPC-0.3 and CPC-0.5 are almost overlapped due to the comparable conductivities and abundant mesoporous structure, demonstrating the robust electrochemical kinetics, as proven by the good rate performance shown in Fig. 3d. For CPC-0, the larger charge-transfer resistance is due to the microporous structure without sufficient ion transport channels. While for CPC-0.8, the reason is due to the low electric conductivity. In addition, the largest slope of the low-frequency line of CPC-0.3 suggests the ideal capacitive behavior with rapid ion migration rate as a result of sufficient ion migration channels.

The CPC-0.3 shows outstanding capacitive and rate performance. To further evaluate its potential for practical applications, the electrochemical performance of CPC-0.3 electrodes with different mass loadings were measured in a three-electrode system. With the mass loadings rising from 5 to 20 mg cm−2, the CV curves are almost overlapped (Fig. 4 a), and the galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles with negligible IR drops are barely distorted (Fig. 4b), indicating the remarkable capacitance retention of CPC-0.3. Importantly, the thick CPC-0.3 electrode at a high mass loading of 20 mg cm−2 still maintains a remarkable capacitance of 142 F g−1 at 10 A g−1 (Fig. 4c), leading to an ultrahigh electrode areal capacitance of 2.8 F cm−2 at 10 A g−1. The rate performance is one of the best among the previously reported carbons (Fig. 4d) [7,25,49–54]. Good electric conductivity and sufficient ion migration channels allow the CPC-0.3 thick electrode maintaining robust electrochemical kinetics at ultrahigh mass loadings, which is clearly seen from the almost overlapped EIS plots in Fig. 4e. From the Bode plots (Fig. 4f), the relaxation time constant (τ0) enlarges from 5.62 to 14.68 s of CPC-0.3 electrodes from 5 to 20 mg cm−2. The CPC-0.3//CPC-0.3 supercapacitor with 2 mg cm−2 mass loading on each electrode exhibits a maximum energy density of 9.21 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 247.75 W kg−1 (Fig. 4g, Fig. S4). Noticeably, even at a very high mass loading of 20 mg cm−2, the energy density is still as high as 6.45 Wh kg−1, which is higher than other carbon materials [53,55,56]. What’s more, the device shows excellent cycle stability with no obvious capacitance fading after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1, indicating the great potential for practical applications.

In this study, a highly conductive mesoporous carbon was prepared through a simple micelle-induced assembly method using sp2 hybridized GQDs as precursors and block copolymer F127 as soft template. This sample shows an interconnected mesoporous structure with both high specific surface area of 1323 m−2 g−1 and good electric conductivity of 73 S m−1, enabling fast electron and ion transport especially at high mass loadings. It shows high capacitances of 315 and 170 F g−1 at 1 and 100 A g−1, respectively. Importantly, at a very high mass loading of 20 mg cm−2, it shows remarkably high areal capacitances of 2.8 F cm−2 at 10 A g−1, which is much better than other reported carbon materials. Moreover, the symmetric supercapacitors show maximum energy densities of 9.21 and 6.45 Wh kg−1 at 2 and 20 mg cm−2, respectively, showing great potential for practical applications.

Wenhui Tian: Writing - original draft. Jiayao Zhu: Writing - original draft. Yue Dong: Writing - original draft. Jing Zhao: Investigation. Jing Li: Investigation. Nannan Guo: Methodology. He Lin: Supervision. Su Zhang: Supervision. Dianzeng Jia: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (2018D01C064).
